title: Elaines Old Friend

jokes.text: Miss Taxi Cab Driver, do you know where I spent my afternoon? In a bar? I was in a bar. And do you... do you know what I was doing? Drinking. I was drinking. You know... you know what I was thinking about? Loneliness? You too, huh?
jokes.setup: Drunk passenger asks a series of leading questions about his sad afternoon
jokes.punchline: Elaine correctly guesses each obvious answer, ending with 'You too, huh?' when she guesses loneliness
jokes.explanation: Decent setup with repetitive structure, the comedy comes from Elaine's weary correctness and the final twist where she admits to the same loneliness

dialogue.speaker: Drunk Passenger
dialogue.line: I had the most terrible afternoon. You want to know what happened?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Sure.

dialogue.speaker: Drunk Passenger
dialogue.line: I went to see my girlfriend. You know what she told me?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: She left you?

dialogue.speaker: Drunk Passenger
dialogue.line: Yeah... Then I went to a bar to drown my sorrows. You know what happened there?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You got drunk?

dialogue.speaker: Drunk Passenger
dialogue.line: Exactly... Then I came here in this cab. You know what I'm feeling right now?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Loneliness?

dialogue.speaker: Drunk Passenger
dialogue.line: Yeah...

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You too, huh?

jokes.text: Are you sure you can find your plane? Listen, if I'm late, they're going to wait for me.
jokes.setup: Elaine expresses concern about a drunk passenger finding his plane
jokes.punchline: The drunk passenger believes the plane will wait for him specifically
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing drunk passenger's inflated self-importance, economical delivery

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: How are you going to find your plane?

dialogue.speaker: Drunk Passenger
dialogue.line: Don't worry about it. They'll wait for me. They always do.

jokes.text: Gimme an E! Gimme an E! Gimme an A-S-T! Gimme an S! Gimme an S! Gimme an I-D-E! East Side, East Side! Go, team, go! Yay! Never heard of it.
jokes.setup: Mary does an elaborate, enthusiastic cheerleader routine for their old high school
jokes.punchline: Elaine's flat 'Never heard of it' response
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure with elaborate setup payoff and perfect deadpan delivery. The contrast between Mary's enthusiasm and Elaine's deflation is well-crafted

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: Give me an E! Give me an L! Give me an A! Give me an I! Give me an N! Give me an E! What's that spell? ELAINE! What's that spell? ELAINE!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Never heard of it.

jokes.text: I must say, I was a little startled to see our homecoming queen driving a cab. Uh, Mary, uh, I wasn't the homecoming queen. Oh, who was? You were. That's right.
jokes.setup: Mary condescendingly mentions being surprised to see the homecoming queen driving a cab
jokes.punchline: Mary realizes she's confused - she was the homecoming queen herself
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment that reveals Mary's unconscious superiority complex and absent-mindedness about her own achievements

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: I'm surprised to see the homecoming queen driving a cab.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: Well, I just assumed... Wait. I was the homecoming queen.

jokes.text: I remember... I wasn't able to be in the senior class play because I was too busy dating.
jokes.setup: Discussing high school achievements and activities
jokes.punchline: Mary's casual humble-brag about being too popular to participate in school activities
jokes.explanation: Establishes Mary's character as someone who casually brags while trying to sound modest

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: I was too popular in high school to do any of those things.

jokes.text: Maybe I should have gone first.
jokes.setup: Mary has just listed all her impressive life achievements
jokes.punchline: Elaine's understated acknowledgment that her life pales in comparison
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy of language to express Elaine's deflation, very relatable moment

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I once got a really good parking spot.

jokes.text: Well... regardless, you... certainly look nice.
jokes.setup: Awkward pause after Elaine's modest life description
jokes.punchline: Mary's condescending consolation prize compliment about looks
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures the awkward condescension of trying to find something nice to say when someone's life is clearly less successful

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: Well, you know, you're very pretty.

jokes.text: Yes, well, I'm even happier than I seem! Happier! Because, uh, you see, I have a steady guy too. Oh, great. Yeah. What's his name? Uh... it's, uh... Billboard. Bill Board.
jokes.setup: Elaine desperately trying to compete with Mary's perfect life, needing a boyfriend name on the spot
jokes.punchline: She improvises the ridiculous name 'Billboard' which she then has to clarify as 'Bill Board'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant moment of panic-driven improvisation that creates an absurd name while perfectly capturing Elaine's desperation to compete

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I have a boyfriend. His name is Billboard.

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: Billboard?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Bill Board. Two words.

jokes.text: Me too.
jokes.setup: Mary says she'd love to meet Elaine's boyfriend
jokes.punchline: Elaine agrees she'd love to meet him too, since he doesn't exist
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - two words that reveal the entire absurdity of the situation. Excellent deadpan delivery of the impossible truth

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: I'd love to meet your boyfriend.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'd love to meet him too. He doesn't exist.

jokes.text: I swear to God, I didn't even floss.
jokes.setup: Elaine describing how depressed she was this morning
jokes.punchline: The ultimate sign of her depression is not flossing
jokes.explanation: Great character-specific detail that shows Elaine's particular brand of neurotic self-care as a depression barometer

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I was so depressed this morning, I didn't even floss.

jokes.text: Does she, have, uh... frizzy blonde hair, granny glasses and smoke French cigarettes? No. Well, if you run into somebody like that, I'd like to meet her.
jokes.setup: Elaine mentions Mary Parker and Tony asks if she matches a specific description
jokes.punchline: When she doesn't match, Tony expresses interest in meeting that other hypothetical woman instead
jokes.explanation: Shows Tony's distractible character and specific taste in women, good misdirection from the main story

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Does she have big breasts and a nice ass?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Then forget Mary Parker. I want to meet the other one.

jokes.text: People you run over are better off than you.
jokes.setup: Alex says it's natural for cab drivers to run into people better off than them
jokes.punchline: Louie's dark twist about running people over instead of running into them
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie - dark, economical, and perfectly timed. The wordplay on 'run into' vs 'run over' is clever and character-appropriate

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I don't run into 'em. I run 'em over.

jokes.text: One of those things to keep you from sticking to your seat.
jokes.setup: Elaine mentioned buying a cushion to keep from sticking to the seat
jokes.punchline: Bobby's interest in this mundane item
jokes.explanation: Sets up what becomes a running gag, shows Bobby's simple character priorities

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: A cushion? What kind of cushion?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Just a regular cushion. For the car seat.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Where'd you get it?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I don't know, some store.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Well, how much did it cost?

jokes.text: What obedience school was that, Bobby?
jokes.setup: Bobby says he was voted 'Most Talented'
jokes.punchline: Louie suggests Bobby went to obedience school like a dog
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie putdown with the implication that Bobby's 'talent' is being obedient like a dog

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Obedience school? You went to obedience school like a dog?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I was voted 'Most Talented'!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well they must've graded on a curve.

jokes.text: That's because you could take your brains out and pass 'em around the room.
jokes.setup: Alex mentions being biology club president
jokes.punchline: Louie's insult about Alex's brain being so small it could be shared
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie insult with creative imagery about small brain size, fits his antagonistic character

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Your brain is so small, it could be shared among three people and none of you would notice the difference.

jokes.text: Blue? I'd like a blue thing to keep me from sticking. It's my favorite color.
jokes.setup: Bobby asked about the seat cushion, Jim now wants in
jokes.punchline: Jim's specific color preference for this mundane item
jokes.explanation: Continues the running gag with Jim's characteristic simple, specific preferences

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I want a yellow one.

jokes.text: There's only... is one thing you need in life and to make... make you happy, and that is friends. That's all you need is friends, and then you be happy.
jokes.setup: Latka tries to cheer up Elaine with life philosophy
jokes.punchline: His earnest but grammatically awkward delivery of simple wisdom
jokes.explanation: Sets up Latka's characteristic speech pattern and begins what becomes an escalating list of life's necessities

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You know, Elaine, is like my country say: when the bird is sad, he must remember he have the wings. The wings, they take him to the sky, and in the sky, there is no sadness, only the clouds and the hope.

jokes.text: You-you also need the food and clothes. You need, you need food and clothes and you need the friends and the food and clothes, then you be happy person.
jokes.setup: Latka's simple philosophy about only needing friends
jokes.punchline: He immediately starts adding basic necessities to the list
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how simple philosophy gets complicated by practical needs

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Who needs anything else? I have my friends.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Well, I need food.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: And shelter.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: And maybe a woman.

jokes.text: So, if you have friends and the food and clothes and the nice car, it is all you need. You don't need anything else. Except a home. You need the home.
jokes.setup: Latka's growing list of life necessities
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts 'you don't need anything else' by adding a home
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation structure with the immediate contradiction of his definitive statement

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You don't need anything else.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Well, maybe a home.

jokes.text: And a beautiful woman, uh, who have a nice body that make you, uh, foam, foaming at the mouth.
jokes.setup: Latka's materialistic list continues
jokes.punchline: His awkward, ESL description of sexual attraction as 'foaming at the mouth'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character-specific language barrier humor with 'foaming at the mouth' as description of attraction

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: And when a man sees a beautiful woman, he gets all... foaming at the mouth. Like a dog with the rabies!

jokes.text: You know, as long as you have the house with the pool, the car and the beautiful woman... the friends would only get in the way. Forget I said anything. I don't know what I'm talking about.
jokes.setup: Latka's materialistic list has grown completely out of hand
jokes.punchline: He realizes he's contradicted his original point about friends being all you need, then gives up entirely
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the escalation sequence. Latka's realization that he's completely contradicted himself and his defeated surrender is excellently structured

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I need friends, I need car, I need apartment, I need stereo, I need color TV, I need VCR, I need microwave, I need hot tub, I need Jacuzzi, I need sauna...

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. I said friends is all you need!

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Forget it. I need a lawyer.

jokes.text: Hey, Elaine, it's that Mary Parker. You know, the woman whose life is so much better than yours.
jokes.setup: Louie answering the phone for Elaine
jokes.punchline: His needlessly cruel but accurate description of Mary when announcing the call
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - unnecessarily cruel truth delivered with characteristic bluntness at the worst possible time

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Mary's on the phone.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Which Mary?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The one who looks like she was put together by a committee.

jokes.text: Hello, Mary, uh, I'm sorry to interrupt. I'd just like to say hello. Hello. Oh, who am I? I'm Elaine's guy.
jokes.setup: Elaine is struggling to make excuses to Mary on the phone
jokes.punchline: Alex suddenly takes the phone and announces himself as her boyfriend
jokes.explanation: Good plot turn and character moment showing Alex stepping in to help Elaine, with natural comedic timing

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Mary, I can't talk right now, I'm... I'm busy.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Hi Mary, this is Alex Rieger, Elaine's boyfriend.

jokes.text: if they can just invent something to keep me from sticking to my shorts.
jokes.setup: The ongoing gag about things to prevent sticking
jokes.punchline: Bobby escalates to a more intimate sticking problem
jokes.explanation: Continues the running gag with appropriate escalation to a more personal/uncomfortable level

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You know what my problem is? I can't get anything to stick.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I tried tape, glue, staples... nothing works.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Even tried rubber cement. My fingers have been stuck together for three days.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: But that's not even the worst part.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Now I can't get my wife to stick around either!

jokes.text: Bill Board? Hey, you came this close to being Karmen Ghia.
jokes.setup: Alex references Elaine's made-up boyfriend name
jokes.punchline: He jokes that she almost used another car-related name (Volkswagen Karmen Ghia)
jokes.explanation: Clever callback that reveals the car billboard inspiration behind 'Bill Board' while suggesting an even more obvious car name

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You almost named him Volkswagen Karmen Ghia.

jokes.text: May I have a glass of water? What's the matter? Your arm's broken?
jokes.setup: Elaine politely asks for water while nervous about the dinner
jokes.punchline: Alex's unnecessarily harsh response about her arm being broken
jokes.explanation: Shows Alex's nervousness coming out as gruffness, classic put-upon response

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Could I please have some water? I'm a little nervous about dinner.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Water? What, is your arm broken?

jokes.text: Well, I don't have as much trouble as my two brothers: Clip and Switch.
jokes.setup: Mary comments on Bill Board being an unusual name
jokes.punchline: Alex invents even more absurd billboard-related brother names
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of the billboard name joke with perfectly plausible-sounding but absurd extensions. Great wordplay and commitment to the bit

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: Board is an unusual last name.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, Bill has two brothers. There's his older brother, Sign Board, and his younger brother, Paste Board.

jokes.text: Well, actually, I was browsing in the Metropolitan Museum of Art one day and, uh, I came upon the most exquisite work of art I've ever seen in my life. I mean, I couldn't take my eyes off her. I just stood there, stunned.
jokes.setup: Michael asks how they met, requiring an improvised romantic story
jokes.punchline: Alex launches into an elaborate, overly romantic museum meet-cute story
jokes.explanation: Shows Alex getting carried away with his improvised romantic story, good character moment of over-commitment to the lie

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: We met at the Metropolitan Museum of Art. I was standing in front of a Monet, completely mesmerized by the way the light played across the water lilies. She came and stood beside me, and without saying a word, she knew exactly what I was feeling. We stood there for what felt like hours, just two souls communicating through the universal language of impressionist art. Then she turned to me and said, 'Isn't it beautiful?' And I said, 'Yes. Yes, it is.' We got married three weeks later.

jokes.text: Oh, two, three years. Three years? Yeah. Time had lost all meaning.
jokes.setup: Alex is asked how long he spent looking for Elaine in museums
jokes.punchline: His absurdly long timeframe and dramatic justification
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the ridiculous romantic story with appropriately dramatic language

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I spent three weeks looking for Elaine in every museum in the city.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I had to. She said she was going to the museum, and I wasn't about to let her get away without saying goodbye.

jokes.text: Me?
jokes.setup: Alex's elaborate story builds to the revelation of who was in the cab
jokes.punchline: Elaine's surprised reaction to being the subject of this over-the-top romantic tale
jokes.explanation: Perfect reaction beat showing Elaine's surprise at being characterized so romantically

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: And there she was, stepping into my cab that fateful evening. Her eyes sparkled like stars reflected in the East River. Her voice was like honey mixed with velvet. Every word she spoke was a symphony to my ears.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Alex, who are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You, Elaine. It was you all along.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Me?! Alex, I just asked you for directions to the bathroom!

jokes.text: We were sunbathing on a beach in the Mediterranean. Cannes. I don't think it was Cannes, dear. Where was it? It was Nice. I've never been to Cannes. Well, dear, it doesn't really matter. It was a beach, right? Yes, dear, it was a beach, but...
jokes.setup: Mary starts her own romantic meeting story
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately corrects her details, creating awkward tension
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing cracks in Mary's perfect relationship through Michael's pedantic corrections

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: So there I was at this coffee shop, and this guy walks in...

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: It was a bagel place.

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: It was a coffee shop. Anyway, he had the most amazing smile...

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: He was grimacing. He had gas.

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: Michael! He asked me out that very moment...

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: You asked him out. He said no twice.

jokes.text: I'd like one of him.
jokes.setup: Waiter asks if they'd like to order, after Alex's romantic declaration
jokes.punchline: Mary's wishful response about wanting a boyfriend like Bill
jokes.explanation: Good timing and economy, shows Mary's dissatisfaction with her own relationship through comparison

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: I wish I had a boyfriend like Bill.

jokes.text: Do you think you could let go of my hand, so I could cut my meat? Oh. Well, I guess you have to eat. I'll give it right back. Promise? Okay.
jokes.setup: Alex is playing the overly romantic boyfriend
jokes.punchline: His commitment to constant hand-holding prevents basic dining functions
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy about over-the-top romantic gestures interfering with practical needs

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I never want to let go of your hand.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's sweet, but how am I supposed to eat?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'll feed you.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: With what? You're holding my other hand.

jokes.text: Jason prayed to God and asked for a train set. Well, the next day, he came to Elaine and he said he'd prayed for a train set and didn't get one. And Elaine was very worried. And she said, 'I'm sorry, Jason, that God didn't answer your prayers.' And Jason said, 'Oh, but he did answer my prayers, Mommy. He just said no.'
jokes.setup: Alex tells a story about Elaine's son praying for a train set
jokes.punchline: The child's wise interpretation that God did answer - with 'no'
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure with good setup and clever theological punchline that shows child's wisdom. Well-constructed anecdote

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: So the kid prays every night for a train set. Christmas morning comes, no train set. He looks up and says, 'Well, God answered my prayers... He said no.'

jokes.text: I didn't remember that story. How did you remember it? I remember everything.
jokes.setup: Alex tells a detailed story about Elaine's son
jokes.punchline: Elaine doesn't remember it, creating awkwardness about a fictional story
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment where the lie threatens to unravel, saved by Alex's quick recovery about remembering everything

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I was at the grocery store yesterday, and I ran into your son. He was telling me about his new job at the architecture firm. He seemed really excited about this project downtown.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: My son? I don't have a son.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What? Sure you do. He was telling me all about it. He said you were very supportive of his career change.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Alex, I don't have any children. I've never had a son.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's... that's weird. He looked just like you. He even had your mannerisms. He kept doing that thing with his hand when he talked.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Alex, you made that up. You're making me very uncomfortable right now.

jokes.text: What is our song, dear? We don't have one.
jokes.setup: Mary asks Michael to dance to 'their song'
jokes.punchline: Michael bluntly reveals they don't have a song
jokes.explanation: Reveals more cracks in Mary's 'perfect' relationship through Michael's unromantic honesty

dialogue.speaker: Mary
dialogue.line: Dance with me to our song.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We don't have a song.

jokes.text: I'm the most exquisite work of art you've ever seen in your life! Oh, that! No, I'd say that to anybody!
jokes.setup: Elaine quotes Alex's romantic description of her back to him
jokes.punchline: Alex's dismissive claim he'd say that to anyone
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the elaborate romantic description with Alex's defensive deflection

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You said my back was a poem.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I say that to everyone.

jokes.text: See? Nothing. Nothing? Nothing.
jokes.setup: Alex claims the kiss meant nothing to prove he wasn't really attracted
jokes.punchline: The obvious contradiction between his claim and his visible reaction
jokes.explanation: Perfect physical comedy moment where Alex's verbal denial contradicts his obvious physical reaction to the kiss

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: It meant nothing. I'm not attracted to you at all.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Then what's that?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What's what?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That.

jokes.text: Well, I better go now. It was nothing. Nothing.
jokes.setup: Alex trying to maintain his denial after the kiss
jokes.punchline: His repeated, unconvincing insistence while clearly flustered
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up showing Alex's continued denial even as he's clearly affected

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: It didn't mean anything. It was just a kiss. People kiss all the time. It was nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I'm not flustered. Why would I be flustered? I kiss people constantly. This is completely normal for me.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Look, it happened, but it doesn't change anything between us. Nothing at all. Everything is exactly the same as it was before.

tmdb_overview: In her cab, Elaine picks up a woman who turns out to be her old high-school rival, Mary. Elaine is embarrassed that she is a cab driver, especially since Mary heads a prestigious ad agency, so to save face, Elaine makes up a story about having a wonderful boyfriend who is a Columbia University professor.  Over the next few days, Mary calls Elaine to try to arrange a dinner with their respective boyfriend's, but Elaine keeps making excuses, as she is ashamed that she made up a lie about having a boyfriend.  When Elaine is about ready to confess her lie to Mary over the phone, Alex grabs the phone, pretending to be Elaine's boyfriend, and accepts her dinner invitation for that evening.  At dinner, Alex is so charming, in an effort to make their relationship look very loving, that all of a sudden, Mary and her boyfriend become jealous that they don't have such an exciting relationship.  Meanwhile, even Elaine is swept up in the moment, and is taken by Alex's romantic acting job.  Having d
insight: Solid character work carries 39 jokes through a middling episode at 1.87 per minute.
